title: The 7% Silent Tax Devouring Your Wealth Every Year

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
The global money supply, measured in dollars, has been expanding at roughly 7% per year for over a century. This relentless expansion finances wars, social programs, government deficits, and every manner of political ambition. The mechanism is not new — it has operated continuously from Babylon and ancient Egypt through colonial America and into the present day. Every colony in pre-revolutionary America experienced bouts of debasement and hyperinflation, and the formation of the United States itself was driven by the need to absorb state debts from serial defaults.

For anyone who doesn't own scarce, desirable assets, this 7% annual debasement functions as a silent tax — a slow erosion of purchasing power that most people never fully recognize. The world's currencies exist in a tiered system: the US dollar debases at around 7%, second-tier currencies like the pound, euro, and yen stagnate slightly faster, third-tier currencies lose roughly 14% annually against scarce assets, and fourth-tier currencies collapse outright.

The practical effect is stark. Land in Miami Beach that cost $10,000 an acre a century ago now costs $10 million. Beachfront property in Palm Beach runs $50 million an acre. Meanwhile, manufactured goods — ketchup, cereal, pencils — get cheaper to produce through automation and efficiency gains, which is why the official CPI hovers around 2%. The divergence between what robots can produce and what is genuinely scarce defines the real inflation experienced by individuals.

This means the hurdle rate for anyone living in the Western world is roughly 7% per year in nominal terms. Savings accounts paying near-zero interest, money markets yielding 3% before tax, and government bonds offering negative real yields are all guaranteed paths to slow impoverishment. The only reliable defense is ownership of assets that appreciate faster than the currency depreciates — property that someone richer and smarter will want to buy from you a decade from now.
